MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the key and recent baselines (skip-gram word2vec, single-Gaussian w2g, and multiple multiprototype methods by Huang, Tian, Chen) across a broad set of standard similarity and entailment datasets, and discusses related work thoroughly. However, the summary lacks evidence that evaluations were run under identical training conditions or that hyperparameters/variance estimates were reported, and it is unclear whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods match or beat their approach. Given comprehensive baseline selection but incomplete details to fully assess fairness and sensitivity, I rate the comparisons as good with minor omissions. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of baselines including skip-gram word2vec, the single-Gaussian w2g (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and several contemporary multiprototype methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and evaluates on a wide range of standard datasets and metrics. However, important reproducibility/fairness details (training corpora/hyperparameter settings and code release) are not reported in the summary, which makes it hard to fully assess whether all models were trained under identical conditions; the paper does acknowledge limitations of particular energy choices (KL) and explicitly positions gains relative to single-Gaussian and skip-gram baselines. Given the breadth of baselines but the missing experimental detail, I rate the comparisons as strong with minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the key and recent baselines (skip-gram word2vec, single-Gaussian w2g, and multiple multiprototype methods by Huang, Tian, Chen) across a broad set of standard similarity and entailment datasets, and discusses related work thoroughly. However, the summary lacks evidence that evaluations were run under identical training conditions or that hyperparameters/variance estimates were reported, and it is unclear whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods match or beat their approach. Given comprehensive baseline selection but incomplete details to fully assess fairness and sensitivity, I rate the comparisons as good with minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of baselines including skip-gram word2vec, the single-Gaussian w2g (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), and several contemporary multiprototype methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and evaluates on a wide range of standard datasets and metrics. However, important reproducibility/fairness details (training corpora/hyperparameter settings and code release) are not reported in the summary, which makes it hard to fully assess whether all models were trained under identical conditions; the paper does acknowledge limitations of particular energy choices (KL) and explicitly positions gains relative to single-Gaussian and skip-gram baselines. Given the breadth of baselines but the missing experimental detail, I rate the comparisons as strong with minor omissions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (probabilistic multi-meaning embeddings via Gaussian mixtures and an analytic energy-based training objective). Notation appears consistent and related work is covered thoroughly; writing quality is reported as good and limitations (e.g. KL for mixtures, numerical stability/initialization) are explicitly acknowledged. Presentation suffers minor practical omissions that matter to reproducibility—hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables fully support the claims, so I deduct one point for these presentation gaps. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Gaussian-mixture word embeddings, the energy-based max-margin objective, and use of the expected likelihood kernel) are stated clearly and early. Notation and related work coverage are reported as thorough, and writing quality is good, making the method and experiments easy to follow. Minor presentation issues remain: practical details such as hyperparameter settings, numerical-stability/initialization recipes, and code availability are not reported, and a few experimental figures/tables could better highlight comparative gains across the many benchmark datasets. From an empirical-modeling perspective the paper communicates its approach and results clearly but would benefit from these additional practical details.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (probabilistic multi-meaning embeddings via Gaussian mixtures and an analytic energy-based training objective). Notation appears consistent and related work is covered thoroughly; writing quality is reported as good and limitations (e.g. KL for mixtures, numerical stability/initialization) are explicitly acknowledged. Presentation suffers minor practical omissions that matter to reproducibility—hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned—and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables fully support the claims, so I deduct one point for these presentation gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (Gaussian-mixture word embeddings, the energy-based max-margin objective, and use of the expected likelihood kernel) are stated clearly and early. Notation and related work coverage are reported as thorough, and writing quality is good, making the method and experiments easy to follow. Minor presentation issues remain: practical details such as hyperparameter settings, numerical-stability/initialization recipes, and code availability are not reported, and a few experimental figures/tables could better highlight comparative gains across the many benchmark datasets. From an empirical-modeling perspective the paper communicates its approach and results clearly but would benefit from these additional practical details.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue given its practically motivated probabilistic embeddings, analytic kernel, and broad empirical evaluation against strong baselines. Reviewer A’s concerns about missing hyperparameter/code details and formal theoretical guarantees are valid reproducibility and methodological caveats but do not change the overall appropriateness for the conference audience. Therefore I rate the paper as an excellent fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: probabilistic representations for word embeddings and the energy-based training using an analytic expected-likelihood kernel are of clear interest to the community and evaluated on standard benchmarks with multiple strong baselines. From my perspective as a theoretical reviewer, I note worrying omissions for a methodology-focused submission — no reported hyperparameters, no code, and no formal guarantees (identifiability/consistency or generalization bounds) — which reduce its appeal for a theory-oriented audience but do not remove its relevance to the venue. Overall it is a good fit, though acceptance would hinge on stronger methodological rigor and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: This work is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it proposes a practically motivated probabilistic embedding (Gaussian mixtures) that explicitly models polysemy, retains analytic tractability via an expected likelihood kernel and an energy-based max-margin training objective, and emphasizes scalability (trained on large corpora). The evaluation is broad and standard for the community (many word similarity and entailment datasets, comparisons to strong baselines including skip-gram, single-Gaussian embeddings, and several multiprototype methods). While the paper notes practical issues (KL intractability for mixtures, numerical stability and initialization), these are implementation concerns rather than a scope mismatch, and the methodological and empirical contributions clearly target the conference audience.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides broad empirical evaluation against appropriate baselines and supports its main modeling claims, but crucial reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) and ablation/sensitivity analyses are missing. Reviewer A additionally flags theoretical and stability/initialization concerns, while Reviewer B is slightly more positive; weighing these views, the substantive empirical contributions are solid but the noted gaps in analyses and reproducibility prevent a higher score.

reviewer_a.justification: The empirical evaluation is reasonably broad (many similarity datasets, SCWS, and an entailment benchmark) and includes several baselines (word2vec, single-Gaussian w2g, and multiple multiprototype methods), which supports the main performance claims. However, the paper lacks critical ablation/sensitivity analyses (fewer than the three distinct ablations I expect to isolate design choices), does not report hyperparameters or provide code, and only partially addresses limitations (mentions KL intractability and training stability but gives few reproducing details). From a theoretical-purist perspective these gaps—missing thorough stability/initialization studies, formal guarantees, and reproducibility details—prevent a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper runs broad empirical comparisons across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and against appropriate baselines (skip‑gram, single Gaussian w2g, and several multiprototype methods), which convincingly supports the main modeling claims. Related work appears thorough and limitations (KL intractability, training stability) are acknowledged. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code reported) and I see no mention of ablation or sensitivity analyses to quantify the contribution of design choices, so some important analyses are left out.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper states clear, testable claims, uses a principled expected-likelihood kernel with closed-form scoring, and presents broad empirical comparisons against strong baselines. They also concur that important practical details (hyperparameters, initialization, code) and additional experimental rigor (statistical tests, ablations/sensitivity analyses) are missing, and theoretical guarantees beyond kernel tractability are not provided. These omissions weaken reproducibility and confidence in the results, so a mid-level score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear main claims and uses a principled energy (expected likelihood kernel) with closed-form evaluation for Gaussian mixtures, and compares against several strong baselines (word2vec, w2g, and multiple multiprototype methods). However, from the summary there are missing theoretical guarantees (no identifiability or generalization bounds), mathematical proofs beyond the kernel tractability are not reported, and critical practical details (hyperparameters, initialization) plus statistical variability and significance tests are absent. I also note insufficient ablation/sensitivity analyses to isolate design choices (fewer than three reported), so while the results are promising they lack the theoretical and experimental rigor I require for higher confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims and supports them with broad empirical evaluation across many standard similarity and entailment datasets and relevant baselines (skip-gram, single-Gaussian, and multiple-prototype methods), and the use of the expected likelihood kernel plausibly gives analytic tractability for Gaussian-mixture energies. The mathematical choice of the kernel and a max-margin energy-based objective is coherent and appears correct for closed-form scoring; the authors also honestly note the KL-for-mixtures limitation. My concerns are practical: critical training details (hyperparameters, stability/initialization recipes) and code availability are not reported, which weakens reproducibility though it does not overturn the empirical findings. Overall the technical claims are well-supported but minor missing experimental details prevent a perfect score.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and delivers consistent empirical improvements with a novel, practical probabilistic multimodal formulation that is likely to influence future work. Because the method is a clear empirical advance with promising directions for adoption despite noted practical and theoretical limitations (KL intractability, stability/initialization, and missing reproducibility details), a slightly above-moderate impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—representing word polysemy probabilistically—by proposing Gaussian-mixture embeddings and an analytically tractable expected likelihood kernel, and demonstrates empirical gains across many standard similarity and entailment benchmarks. However, from my theoretical-purist perspective the contribution falls short of changing how the field reasons about representation learning: it lacks formal guarantees (identifiability/consistency or generalization bounds), detailed ablations and hyperparameter reporting, and has acknowledged limitations (no closed-form KL for mixtures, stability/initialization issues). The method is a useful, scalable empirical advance likely to be built upon, but without stronger principled analysis and reproducibility details its impact is moderate rather than transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important and well-known problem of polysemy in word representations by proposing the first probabilistic Gaussian-mixture embeddings and an analytically tractable energy (expected likelihood) that preserves scalability—an appealing combination for both researchers and practitioners. Empirically, the method demonstrates consistent improvements over single-Gaussian and skip-gram baselines across a wide range of similarity and entailment benchmarks (e.g., SimLex, SCWS, Baroni et al.), which makes it likely to influence modeling choices. The probabilistic, multimodal formulation and closed-form kernel open clear new directions for uncertainty-aware and sense-aware representation work, and the approach is reasonably likely to be adopted or extended in downstream representation learning. It stops short of being fully transformative because practical issues (e.g., KL intractability, stability/initialization) and missing details (code/hyperparameters) may slow immediate widespread uptake.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the work makes a substantive and non-trivial contribution by introducing a probabilistic, multimodal (mixture-of-Gaussians) word representation with a closed-form expected-likelihood kernel, distinguishing it from prior single-Gaussian and non-probabilistic multiprototype methods. While Reviewer A frames it as notable rather than revolutionary, the combination of addressing polysemy probabilistically and retaining analytic tractability is a clear technical advance, warranting the top originality score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and non-trivial extension of prior probabilistic embeddings (w2g) by introducing Gaussian mixture word representations and an expected-likelihood energy that retains analytic tractability for mixtures. This direction meaningfully differentiates from prior multiprototype (Huang, Tian, Chen) and single-Gaussian (Vilnis & McCallum) work by providing the first probabilistic mixture model for polysemy rather than just multiple point vectors, and the closed-form kernel is a substantive technical contribution. From a theoretical-purist perspective it is not a radical reconceptualization of representation learning (it builds directly on established Gaussian-embedding and multiprototype ideas), so I judge it notable novelty rather than a breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: From the provided summary, the paper appears to introduce a genuinely new modeling perspective: a fully probabilistic, multimodal word representation (mixture-of-Gaussians) trained with a max-margin objective using the expected likelihood kernel to retain closed-form tractability. This clearly distinguishes it from prior single-Gaussian (w2g) and non-probabilistic multiprototype methods (Huang et al., Tian et al., Chen et al.), and addresses polysemy while preserving analytic efficiency — a nontrivial technical advance that would inform experts in embedding research.

main_claims: Proposes the first probabilistic word embedding that can capture multiple meanings by representing each word as a Gaussian mixture.
Introduces an energy-based max-margin training objective using the expected likelihood kernel to retain analytic tractability and scalability for Gaussian mixture embeddings.

Shows that the Gaussian mixture model (w2gm) reduces variance for polysemous words and yields improved performance over single-Gaussian embeddings and skip-gram baselines on word similarity and entailment benchmarks.
_summary.methodology: Represent words as mixtures of Gaussians and train all mixture parameters with a maximum-margin energy-based ranking objective; use the expected likelihood kernel (inner product between distributions) as the energy function, allowing closed-form evaluation for Gaussian mixtures.
datasets: Baroni et al. (2012) word entailment dataset
(training corpora mentions: 5 billion tokens and Wackypedia (1 billion tokens))
metrics: KL divergence (used as a similarity/entailment score)
limitations: KL divergence does not have a closed form for two Gaussian mixtures (stated as a limitation of that energy choice).
Numerical stability and initialization require critical practical details (training requires care for stability and initialization).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - word embeddings / distributional semantic representations

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a focus on probabilistic modeling and statistical learning theory; long experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICLR.
traits: Penalises papers that present empirical improvements without accompanying formal guarantees (e.g., no generalization bounds, identifiability, or consistency proofs).
Requires clear ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (will deduct points if fewer than 3 ablations that isolate main design choices).
Demands strong and comprehensive baselines (expects at least 4 established baselines or clear justification for omission).
Insists on reporting statistical significance or variability (will penalise if confidence intervals or proper variance estimates are missing).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises theoretical clarity and methodological guarantees over empirical gains; awards points only when empirical work is buttressed by principled analysis and comprehensive ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Modeling Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher in NLP and representation learning with extensive experience building and evaluating embedding models for downstream tasks; regular reviewer for ACL and EMNLP.
traits: Rewards novel, practically useful modeling ideas that address known limitations (gives positive bias to methods that explicitly model polysemy/multimodality).
Favors empirical breadth and demonstrable improvements on standard benchmarks (will upscore if the method shows consistent gains across multiple evaluation sets).
Values scalability and closed-form/efficient computations (gives credit when a probabilistic formulation maintains analytic tractability and can scale to large vocabularies).
Prefers probabilistic and interpretable representations (will positively score methods that provide uncertainty estimates or interpretable mixture components).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on empirical contribution, practical impact, and scalability; willing to accept less theoretical depth when a model demonstrates clear empirical advances and usability.
